title: Consumer Theory & Choice
description: Utility, scarcity, opportunity cost, consumer equilibrium, and the law of diminishing marginal utility

questions.statement: Utility means the objective usefulness of a good.
questions.payload: Utility is subjective **satisfaction**, not objective usefulness.

questions.statement: A cigarette has utility for a smoker even though it is harmful.
questions.payload: Correct. Utility depends on **personal preference**, not health or usefulness.

questions.statement: Total utility reaches its maximum when marginal utility becomes zero.
questions.payload: Correct. When MU is zero, consuming more adds **no satisfaction** and TU peaks.

questions.statement: Total utility falls whenever marginal utility is falling.
questions.payload: TU still rises as long as MU is **positive**, even if MU is falling.

questions.statement: Marginal utility can become negative.
questions.payload: Correct. Beyond a certain point, additional units **reduce** total satisfaction.

questions.statement: Cardinal utility assumes satisfaction can be measured in exact numbers called utils.
questions.payload: Correct. Cardinal utility treats satisfaction as **quantifiable** in precise units.

questions.statement: Ordinal utility requires that we measure satisfaction in exact numerical units.
questions.payload: Ordinal utility only **ranks** preferences as first, second, or third.

questions.statement: Both cardinal and ordinal utility assume the consumer is rational.
questions.payload: Correct. Both approaches rely on the assumption that consumers make **consistent** choices.

questions.statement: Scarcity and shortage mean exactly the same thing in economics.
questions.payload: Scarcity is **permanent** and universal, while shortage is temporary and specific.

questions.statement: Opportunity cost is the money spent on the chosen good.
questions.payload: Opportunity cost is the value of the **next-best alternative** forgone, not the money spent.

questions.statement: Even wealthy countries face the problem of scarcity.
questions.payload: Correct. Scarcity is permanent because human wants are **unlimited** and resources are finite.

questions.statement: A rational consumer stops consuming when marginal utility becomes negative.
questions.payload: Correct. No rational consumer buys a unit that **reduces** total satisfaction.

questions.statement: At consumer equilibrium, the marginal utility per birr spent is equal for all goods purchased.
questions.payload: Correct. A rational consumer allocates income so that **MUx divided by Px equals MUy divided by Py**.

questions.statement: If MUx divided by Px is greater than MUy divided by Py, the consumer should buy less of good X.
questions.payload: The consumer should buy **more** of good X until the ratios are equal.

questions.statement: The law of diminishing marginal utility states that total utility decreases with each additional unit consumed.
questions.payload: The law states that **marginal** utility decreases, not total utility.

questions.statement: A consumer with a budget of 10 birr can be in equilibrium while spending only 8 birr.
questions.payload: Consumer equilibrium requires the **entire budget** to be spent.

questions.statement: Cardinal utility theory assumes diminishing marginal utility.
questions.payload: Correct. Cardinal utility explicitly assumes that each additional unit adds **less** satisfaction.

questions.statement: Ordinal utility theory does not assume diminishing marginal utility.
questions.payload: Correct. Ordinal utility relies on ranking preferences, **not** on diminishing marginal utility.

questions.statement: The first cup of coffee in an Ethiopian coffee ceremony gives the highest marginal utility.
questions.payload: Correct. The law of diminishing marginal utility means the **first** unit typically yields the greatest extra satisfaction.

questions.statement: Shortage is a permanent condition caused by unlimited wants and limited resources.
questions.payload: Shortage is **temporary** and market-specific; scarcity is the permanent condition.
